intro.title: Articles: a, an, the and no article

sections.title: What articles do (core logic)
sections.content:
Before almost every noun in English you make a three-way choice: **a / an** (indefinite), **the** (definite), or **∅** (no article). The speaker is signalling one thing to the listener: **"Is this thing identifiable to you or not?"**

• **a / an** = singular countable, **not yet identifiable** (first mention, one of many): *I bought **a** car.*
• **the** = **identifiable** to both speaker and listener (already mentioned, unique, or specified by context): *The sun. The car I bought.*
• **∅** = used with **uncountables and plurals in general**, names, most countries: *Water is essential. Cats are animals. Prague.*

**Why it matters (roots-first)**: Articles sit in the Noun Phrase root (#3 in the Tree Model). They are the **most frequent grammatical decision in English** — you make it almost every time you open your mouth, several times per sentence. Czech has **no articles at all**, which is why this is the single hardest English structure for Czech speakers to acquire. The good news: ~80% of cases are covered by the core rules below.

B2 exceptions, idiomatic uses and edge cases (abstract nouns generic vs specific, *the rich / the poor*, rivers and mountains, newspaper and hotel names) live in a separate topic: [[topic:articles_advanced|Articles — extension]]. Master the core first.

diagram.title: The three-way choice at a glance
rows: Singular countable, first mention, one of many
Listener can identify which one (mentioned, unique, specified)
**The** sun. **The** car I bought is red.
Plural / uncountable in general, most names
Cats are animals. Go to work. Prague.

sections.title: a / an — first mention, indefinite, "one of many"
sections.content:
Use **a / an** with a **singular countable noun** when the listener doesn't yet know which one — there are many possibilities, and you're picking one.

• **First mention** in a story or conversation: *I bought **a** book yesterday.*
• **One of many** (profession, type, category): *She's **a** teacher. That's **a** Honda.*
• **One per** something: *Take **a** pill three times **a** day.*

**a or an? Listen to the SOUND, not the letter.**

**Why it matters**: This is rule one of every spoken English exchange — introducing a new thing into the conversation. Get the *a / an* sound rule right and you sound natural from sentence one. Get it wrong and every listener notices.

**Contrast examples** (sound, not spelling):
- **a** university, **a** European country, **a** one-way street (start with *yoo-*, *yur-*, *wun-* → consonant sound)
- **an** hour, **an** honest man (silent *h* → vowel sound)
- **an** MBA, **an** FBI agent, **an** X-ray (letter names *em-*, *ef-*, *ex-* start with vowel sounds)

sections.title: the — definite, identifiable, "we both know which one"
sections.content:
Use **the** when both speaker and listener can identify **which one**. Three main triggers:

• **(a) Already mentioned** (second-mention rule — see next card): *I saw a dog. **The** dog was barking.*
• **(b) Only one in context** — unique by nature or by situation: ***The** sun. **The** moon. **The** manager. Close **the** door.* (this room's door)
• **(c) Defined by what follows** — a phrase or clause that pins it down: ***The** book I bought yesterday. **The** capital of France. **The** water in this bottle.*

Also: superlatives (***the** best, **the** first*), and some place names with common-noun elements (***the** USA, **the** UK, **the** Czech Republic*).

**Why it matters**: Czech speakers tend to under-use *the*. Every time you refer back to something already known, English requires a signal — *the* is that signal. Missing it doesn't usually break meaning, but it sounds noticeably foreign in every email, exam answer and conversation.

**Pronunciation tip**: *the* is pronounced **"thee"** before a vowel sound (*thee apple, thee hour*) and **"thuh"** before a consonant sound (*thuh book, thuh university*).

sections.title: No article (∅) — general, abstract, plural, uncountable
sections.content:
Use **no article** (∅) when you're talking about something in **general**, not a specific instance.

• **(a) Uncountable nouns in general**: ∅ *Water is essential. ∅ Information is power.*
• **(b) Plural countable nouns in general**: ∅ *Dogs are friendly. ∅ Children need sleep.*
• **(c) Abstract nouns in general**: ∅ *Love is patient. ∅ Time flies.*
• **(d) Names**: ∅ *Mary, ∅ Prague, ∅ France, ∅ Japan, ∅ January.* (Most personal names, cities, countries, months.)
• **(e) Set categories**: meals (*have ∅ breakfast*), transport with *by* (*by ∅ car, by ∅ train*), days/months alone (*see you on ∅ Monday*), institutions as activity (*go to ∅ work, at ∅ home, go to ∅ school*).

**Why it matters**: This is the rule Czech speakers find most natural — Czech also says these without an article. **But beware:** the moment the noun becomes specific (defined by context or a following phrase), English switches to *the*.

**Contrast** — the most important pair in this topic:
- ∅ ***Water** is essential.* (water in general — uncountable, general → ∅)
- ***The water** in this bottle is cold.* (this specific water → the)
- ∅ ***Dogs** bark.* (dogs in general → ∅)
- ***The dogs** next door bark all night.* (these specific dogs → the)

sections.title: a/an → the (the storytelling rule)
sections.content:
**The most fundamental discourse pattern in English**: a thing is **a/an** the first time you mention it, then **the** every time after — because now we both know which one you mean.

• *I saw **a** dog in the park. **The** dog was chasing **a** squirrel. **The** squirrel ran up **a** tree. **The** tree was huge.*

Each new noun enters with *a/an* (or ∅ for plurals/uncountables), then graduates to *the* on the next mention. This is how English stories, news articles, emails and conversations are knitted together.

**Why it matters**: This single pattern is behind a huge proportion of *a* vs *the* decisions. Every email you write, every exam essay, every story you tell uses it constantly. If you only remember **one rule** about articles, remember this one.

**Contrast chain**:
- *I need **a** new phone.* (first mention → a)
- *Did you see **the** phone I bought?* (now identifiable → the)
- *Yes, **the** screen is huge!* (part of the known phone → the)

The same logic works with plurals/uncountables: first mention with ∅, then *the* once known.
- *I bought ∅ **flowers** for my mother. **The** flowers were beautiful.*

sections.title: Articles with names — quick guide
sections.content:
Most **names** take no article — but a small, learnable set takes **the**.

**∅ No article** (the default for names):
- Personal names: ∅ *John, ∅ Mary, ∅ Mr Novák*
- Cities: ∅ *Prague, ∅ London, ∅ New York*
- Most countries: ∅ *Spain, ∅ Japan, ∅ France, ∅ Germany*
- Continents: ∅ *Europe, ∅ Africa, ∅ Asia*
- Streets: ∅ *Wenceslas Square, ∅ Oxford Street*
- Single mountains and lakes (with their generic word): ∅ *Mount Everest, ∅ Lake Geneva*

**With "the"** (learnable categories):
- Countries with a common noun in the name: ***the** UK, **the** USA, **the** Czech Republic, **the** Netherlands*
- Rivers, seas, oceans: ***the** Vltava, **the** Thames, **the** Atlantic, **the** Mediterranean*
- Mountain ranges and groups of islands: ***the** Alps, **the** Himalayas, **the** Canary Islands*
- A few set expressions: ***the** Hague, **the** Bronx*

**Why it matters**: Saying *"I live in Czech Republic"* or *"I went to the London"* are two of the most instantly noticeable Czech-speaker errors. Fixing this gives the most visible quick win.

*(Newspapers, hotels, ships, institutions, and the full geography list are in [[topic:articles_advanced|Articles — extension]].)*

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors (and roadmap)
sections.content:
Because Czech has no articles, five mistakes appear constantly. Fix these and your English improves visibly within a week.

1. **Dropping articles altogether** — *I went to ∅ shop* → **I went to the shop.** / *She's ∅ doctor* → **She's a doctor.**
2. **Over-using "the" with generics** — *~~The~~ cats are animals* → **Cats are animals.** / *I like ~~the~~ music* → **I like music.** (in general)
3. **a/an based on letter, not sound** — *~~a~~ hour* → **an hour.** / *~~an~~ university* → **a university.**
4. **Wrong article with countries** — *~~The~~ France is beautiful* → **France is beautiful.** / *I live in ~~∅~~ Czech Republic* → **I live in the Czech Republic.**
5. **First / subsequent mention confusion** — *I bought ~~the~~ car yesterday* (introducing for the first time) → **I bought a car yesterday.**

**Why it matters**: These are the exact mistakes that mark out a Czech speaker in every email, exam answer and conversation. They're not difficult — they're just unfamiliar. Practice the cards in this topic, then move on.

---

**Direct practice in this curriculum**:
- Choose a / an / the / ∅ (MC)
- Gap fill: articles
- Error correction: fix the wrong article

**Next steps**: When the core feels automatic, move to [[topic:articles_advanced|Articles — extension]] (B2: abstract nouns generic vs specific, *the rich/the poor*, full geography list, newspaper/hotel/ship names, subjects of study, title vs role).

questions.question: 'I need _____ umbrella.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: Umbrella starts with a vowel sound → an.

questions.question: '_____ life is short.' (general) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Life in general (uncountable) → no article.

questions.question: 'I bought a car. _____ car is red.' Which fits in the second sentence?
questions.explanation: Second mention – we know which car → the.

questions.question: 'She goes to _____ work by bus.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: Go to work (as activity) → no article.

questions.question: 'We had _____ breakfast at eight.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: Meals in general (have breakfast) → no article.

questions.question: 'She dreams of visiting _____ Africa.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: Continents take no article → Africa, Europe, Asia.

gapfill.title: Practice: Choose a, an, the or no article (∅)

questions.question: I need _____ umbrella.
questions.explanation: Vowel sound (umbrella) → an.

questions.question: She's _____ engineer.
questions.explanation: Vowel sound (engineer) → an. One of many.

questions.question: _____ sun is very bright today.
questions.explanation: Unique – there is only one sun → the.

questions.question: We had _____ breakfast at eight.
questions.explanation: Meals in general → no article.

questions.question: He goes to _____ work by bus.
questions.explanation: Go to work (activity) → no article.

questions.question: I bought _____ new car. _____ car is red.
questions.explanation: First mention → a. Second mention (we know which one) → the.

questions.question: _____ cats are animals.
questions.explanation: Plural in general → no article.

questions.question: She has _____ hour for lunch.
questions.explanation: Hour starts with vowel sound (silent h) → an.

questions.question: Close _____ door, please.
questions.explanation: The door in this room – listener knows which one → the.

questions.question: They live in _____ USA.
questions.explanation: Country name that includes a common noun (United States) → the.

questions.question: _____ water in the bottle is cold.
questions.explanation: Specific water (in that bottle) → the.

questions.question: It's _____ best film I've ever seen.
questions.explanation: Superlative (best) → the.

questions.question: _____ life is full of surprises.
questions.explanation: Life in general (uncountable) → no article.

questions.question: I'll meet you at _____ home.
questions.explanation: At home (fixed phrase) → no article.

questions.question: She dreams of visiting _____ Africa.
questions.explanation: Continents take no article → Africa, Europe, Asia.

questions.question: Wrong: 'The cats are animals.' (meaning all cats) Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Plural in general → no article.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I went to the London.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Most city names → no article.

questions.question: Wrong: 'She goes to the work by bus.' Correct the sentence.
answers: She goes to work by bus.
She goes to work by bus
questions.explanation: Go to work (activity) → no article.

questions.question: Wrong: 'We had a breakfast at eight.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Meals in general (have breakfast) → no article.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I need a umbrella.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Vowel sound (umbrella) → an.

questions.question: Wrong: 'He is an university student.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: University starts with consonant sound (yoo-) → a.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Sun is very bright today.' Correct the sentence.
answers: The sun is very bright today.
The sun is very bright today
questions.explanation: Unique (the sun) → the.

questions.question: Wrong: 'They live in USA.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: USA (country name with common noun) → the.

questions.question: Wrong: 'He visited the Africa.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Continents take no article → Africa, Europe, Asia.

mc.title: Practice: Choose a, an, the or no article (∅) (multiple choice)

questions.explanation: Vowel sound (umbrella) → an.

questions.question: She's _____ engineer.
questions.explanation: Vowel sound (engineer) → an. One of many.

questions.question: _____ sun is very bright today.

questions.question: We had _____ breakfast at eight.
questions.explanation: Meals in general → no article.

questions.question: He goes to _____ work by bus.
questions.explanation: Go to work (activity) → no article.

questions.question: I bought _____ new car. _____ car is red.

questions.question: _____ cats are animals.
questions.explanation: Plural in general → no article.

questions.question: She has _____ hour for lunch.
questions.explanation: Hour starts with vowel sound (silent h) → an.

questions.question: Close _____ door, please.
questions.explanation: The door in this room – listener knows which one → the.

questions.question: They live in _____ USA.
questions.explanation: Country name that includes a common noun (United States) → the.
